elements.sent-2411-001: Understanding the fracture mechanisms in composite materials across scales, from nano- to micro-scales, is essential for an in-depth understanding of the reinforcement mechanisms and designing the next generation of lightweight, high-strength composites.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, conventional methods struggle to model the complex fracture behavior of nanocomposites, particularly at the fiber-matrix interface.
elements.sent-2411-003: The phase-field regularized cohesive fracture model has proven to be effective in simulating crack initiation, branching, and propagation; however, capturing the cohesive fracture strength at smaller scales remains a significant challenge.
elements.sent-2411-004: This study introduces a novel approach that combines an energy-based star-convex decomposition cohesive phase-field fracture model with molecular dynamics simulations to explore the thickness dependency of nanocomposite mechanical properties.
elements.sent-2411-005: The proposed framework enables hierarchical modeling of carbon-nitride nanosheet-reinforced composites' mechanical and fracture behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-006: The developed model could elucidate complex fracture processes across different scales and highlight critical scaling effects.
elements.sent-2411-007: This methodology provides an efficient solution for uncovering hierarchical fracture mechanisms in reinforced nanocomposites, offering valuable insights into their fracture behavior and strengthening mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-008: Since the first mechanical exfoliation of graphene \cite{novoselov2004electric,geim2007rise,schedin2007detection,neto2009electronic in 2004 and the synthesis of g-$\rm{C_{3 N_{4 }$ \cite{liu2015metal in 2009, two-dimensional materials such as graphene (GN) and carbon-nitride ($\text{C _{x}\text{N _{y}$) nanosheets with few-layer thicknesses have attracted significant attraction in both academy and industry.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Among these materials, graphene plays a pioneering role in the field of two-dimensional materials due to its simple lattice structure and remarkable properties, including thermal conductivity \cite{shahil2012graphene,balandin2008superior , mechanical strength \cite{lee2008measurement , and electronic characteristics \cite{neto2009electronic .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: These outstanding physical properties have enabled graphene to be extensively attractive as a functional component to enhance properties of composites across various scales, such as nanoscale microscale, and even the macroscale \cite{dong2021physicochemical .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Recently, several investigations \cite{hatam2020thermal,rajabpour2019carbon have highlighted that experimentally synthesized $\rm{C_{3 N}$ \cite{mahmood2016two exhibits comparable mechanical properties and thermal conductivity similar to graphene, while also possessing a more stable structure compared to other carbon-nitride monolayers, These studies highlight the remarkable properties and practical applications of both GN and $\text{C _{x}\text{N _{y}$ across a wide range of industrial sectors.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The key aspect that leads to the different mechanical and thermal transport properties of composite systems and heterostructures at different scales is related to the dimensions of the fillers.
elements.sent-2411-013: At the nanoscale or in nanocomposites, the surface area between the fillers and the matrix per volume is much higher compared to the microcomposites.
elements.sent-2411-014: In order to simulate and capture the corresponding interfacial properties, it is crucial to first investigate the atomic interactions between the fillers and the matrix, which are usually polymeric systems.
elements.sent-2411-015: This is usually realized by incorporating and carefully analyzing the non-banding van der Waals interactions.
elements.sent-2411-016: At the nanoscale, van der Waals interactions have been widely utilized to model cohesive interactions among atoms.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, the van der Waals-driven multiscale cohesive fracture behavior of $\text{C _{x}\text{N _{y}$-reinforced composites remain underexplored in the existing research literature.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: The complexity of fracture criteria, along with challenges in mitigating information loss during cross-scale modeling, has created significant gaps in understanding the multiscale fracture response of both GN and $\text{C _{x}\text{N _{y}$, particularly at the atomic and continuum levels.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: Moreover, the investigation of the fracture behavior of the pristine carbon-nitride monolayer, which has a thickness of only a few angstroms (\AA), presents a significant challenge in the experimental setting.
elements.sent-2411-020: To overcome these challenges, molecular dynamics (MD) simulations combined with multiscale modeling provide a promising approach for investigating the mechanical and fracture behaviors of carbon-nitride materials.
elements.sent-2411-021: In the context of multiscale modeling, various methods offer frameworks to thoroughly investigate complex physical behaviors across different scales.
elements.sent-2411-022: These approaches are generally classified into three main categories: hierarchical, semi-concurrent, and concurrent methods \cite{talebi2014computational, budarapu2019multiscale .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Among the aforementioned multiscale methods, the hierarchical multiscale method is capable of transferring and bridging information from lower to upper scales.
elements.sent-2411-024: However, it can not perform a reverse transfer.
elements.sent-2411-025: This method's high computational efficiency makes it particularly suitable for hierarchically modeling new two-dimensional materials, such as investigating the mechanical properties of graphene/borophene (GN/BN) heterostructures and graphene-reinforced composites.
elements.sent-2411-026: In contrast, the semi-concurrent multiscale method focuses on bi-directional information exchange between lower and upper scales.
elements.sent-2411-027: A typical example is the $\rm{FE^2}$ method \cite{feyel1999multiscale,feyel2000fe2 which exemplifies this approach.}
elements.sent-2411-028: However, the $\rm{FE^2}$ method faces significant computational challenges due to the parallel computations required at both the micro and macro scales.
elements.sent-2411-029: This limitation restricts its application primarily to simple, two-dimensional academic examples.
elements.sent-2411-030: As a result, it is less effective in accurately representing the complex characteristics of composite materials, such as GN or $\text{C _{x}\text{N _{y}$ platelet reinforcements.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Additionally, the $\rm{FE^2}$ method suffers from instability in cases of material softening, which can result from damage or other degradation mechanisms \cite{budarapu2019multiscale .}
elements.sent-2411-032: At the macroscale, classical continuum mechanics effectively models the fracture behavior of materials using several degradation functions to degrade material stiffness in order to realize the damage and fracture of materials \cite{danesh2021comparative,danesh2022free .}
elements.sent-2411-033: However, it exhibits limited capability to describe the initiation and propagation of cracks arising from atomic dislocations at the nanoscale.
elements.sent-2411-034: This limitation arises from the difference in modeling mechanisms between continuum mechanics and molecular dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-035: In continuum mechanics, partial differential equations (PDEs) govern the equilibrium conditions within a continuous medium and are typically solved using discretization techniques, like finite elements method \cite{wriggers2008nonlinear,miehe2010phase,miehe2010thermodynamically , meshfree free method \cite{rabczuk2019extended,ren2016dual,ren2017dual , etc. However, at the nanoscale, material deformations are governed by atomic dislocations.}}
elements.sent-2411-036: Additionally, accurately capturing the fracture process presents substantial challenges for conventional continuum mechanics, where the de-bonding of individual atoms at the crack tips leads to the formation of microscopic voids (damage).
elements.sent-2411-037: This study aims to explore the thickness-dependent fracture mechanisms of GN and other prominent $\text{C _{x}\text{N _{y}$ nanosheets reinforced polymer composites, by developing a state-of-the-art hierarchical multiscale method.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: This method starts from the natural properties of materials, circumventing the challenges typically associated with the natural discontinuity of cracks on the RVE boundary by a displacement jump.
elements.sent-2411-039: This enables a more focused exploration of the material properties.
elements.sent-2411-040: However, the complexity and discontinuity of material fracture modeling require diverse evolution criteria.
elements.sent-2411-041: To address this issue, the phase-field fracture model provides a flexible and elegant solution.
elements.sent-2411-042: For modeling crack propagation within RVEs at the microscale, the cohesive phase-field fracture model \cite{wu2017unified,rezaei2022anisotropic is preferred rather than the displacement jump-based cohesive zone models \cite{rezaei2017prediction,tarafder2020finite .}}
elements.sent-2411-043: This preference stems from the strong capability of the phase-field model to simulate crack initiation, propagation, coalescence, branching \cite{zhuang2022phase,zhou2019phase , and phase transformations \cite{danesh2022thermodynamically,danesh2021nonlocal .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Furthermore, the cohesive zone model suffers from discontinuities within the fracture zone and significantly relies on predefined duplicate nodes in the fracture zone.
elements.sent-2411-045: To address these limitations, this study employs a hybrid approach that combines a cohesive phase-field model and molecular dynamics method.
elements.sent-2411-046: The combined approach aims to reveal the crack patterns of graphene and carbon-nitride as well as the fracture behavior of their reinforced composites over multiple scales.
elements.sent-2411-047: On account of the above issues, the content of this study is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-048: In \hyperref[Sec2: MD and CPF]{{Section 2 , details of the classical molecular dynamics modeling developed to acquire the mechanical and fracture responses of the considered monolayers (referred to as fibers), cohesive interfaces, and polymer matrix at the atomic scale are provided.}}
elements.sent-2411-049: Next, the homogenization theory developed to facilitate the bridge between the nanoscale and microscale is given.
elements.sent-2411-050: Subsequently, the isotropic/anisotropic cohesive phase-field fracture theory is presented at the microscale.
elements.sent-2411-051: Finally, \hyperref[Sec5: Conclusion]{{Section 5 provides concluding remarks and outlook.}}

elements.sent-2411-268: Following an in-depth analysis of fracture behavior in RVEs, investigating both cohesive and non-cohesive factors is essential to deeply understand the mechanical and fracture responses of graphene and carbon nitride-reinforced composites.
elements.sent-2411-269: Key factors such as fiber/matrix interfacial strength and fiber loading orientation significantly influence the fracture strength of micro- and nano-fiber-reinforced composites.
elements.sent-2411-270: Accordingly, a comparative study of the homogenized UTS between cohesive and non-cohesive (standard AT2) models is presented in the following section.

elements.sent-2411-279: Another critical phenomenon in multiscale modeling is the scaling effect, which is observed in simulations of thermal conductivity and mechanical properties , as well as in experiments conducted by \citet{malekpour2014thermal .}
elements.sent-2411-280: As illustrated in \hyperref[fig9]{Fig.10 (a) , the scaling effect is significant in both the homogenized UTS and elastic modulus for the cohesive fracture model, spanning from the nanoscale to the microscale.}
elements.sent-2411-281: When comparing scales of 1 nm, 5 nm, 10 nm, and 1 $\rm{\mu m}$ at a consistent volume fraction, the UTS of the graphene-reinforced composites decreases as the scale size decreases from 1 $\mu$m to 1 nm.
elements.sent-2411-282: Interestingly, \hyperref[fig9]{Fig.10 (a) shows that the primary scaling effect on the UTS occurs between 1 nm and 10 nm, with mean differences of $45.78\%$ between 1 nm and 5 nm as well as $22.54\%$ between 5 nm and 10 nm.}
elements.sent-2411-283: This substantial variation across scales underscores the importance of carefully considering the scaling effect when modeling and designing composite materials from small to large scales.
elements.sent-2411-284: Thus, it is essential to recognize that the performance of composite materials is influenced not only by the complex physical properties of the fibers, matrix, and interface but also by the scaling effect on the UTS across nanoscale to microscale dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-285: In addition to the UTS, the homogenized elastic modulus is essential for characterizing the mechanical behavior of composites.
elements.sent-2411-286: As depicted in \hyperref[fig9]{Fig.10 (b) , the scaling effect on the homogenized elastic modulus is minimal, with the elastic modulus at the nanoscale only marginally lower than at the microscale.}
elements.sent-2411-287: This difference can typically be disregarded when the fiber volume fraction is relatively low (below $2\%$).
elements.sent-2411-288: Furthermore, comparisons between the cohesive and non-cohesive models at a scale of one micrometer reveal negligible differences in the elastic modulus.
elements.sent-2411-289: This observation can be attributed to the fact that the composite’s elastic modulus is assessed under relatively low deformation, where insufficient strain prevents interface separation.
elements.sent-2411-290: Consequently, it is reasonable to conclude that the non-bond (van der Waals) strength-induced cohesive interaction does not significantly influence Young's modulus.
elements.sent-2411-291: Although a monotonic increase in the modulus is observed while increasing fiber volume fraction, the effect of the interfacial cohesive interaction on the modulus remains insignificant.
elements.sent-2411-292: Thus, while the scaling effect substantially influences the UTS of composites as the scale of dimension decreases from one micrometer to one nanometer, its impact on the homogenized elastic modulus is relatively minor.
elements.sent-2411-293: This study presents a combined hierarchical molecular dynamics and phase-field multiscale modeling approach to investigate the fracture behavior of nanocomposites from the nano- to the microscale.
elements.sent-2411-294: We investigated the scaling effects and fracture responses of polymer composites reinforced by graphene and carbon nitride nanomembranes.
elements.sent-2411-295: By integrating molecular dynamics with an anisotropic star-convex cohesive phase-field model, this approach captures the intrinsic van der Waals forces driving cohesive interactions, from nanoscale interactions to macroscale fracture behavior.
elements.sent-2411-296: The simulation results revealed a remarkable scaling effect: the tensile strength of the composites increased nonlinearly with the filler thickness, especially for graphene-reinforced composites with filler thicknesses ranging from 1 nm to 10 nm.
elements.sent-2411-297: Our multiscale modeling results indicate that for thin nanosheets, the fiber-matrix interface strength plays a dominant role in enhancing fracture resistance, whereas for thicker fillers, fracture resistance is influenced by the reinforcement distribution and density, leading to a softening effect.
elements.sent-2411-298: Furthermore, the star-convex cohesive phase-field model successfully splits the asymmetric fracture response between tension and compression, allowing the simulation of damage evolution for complex RVEs.
elements.sent-2411-299: This approach highlights the critical influence of fiber-induced cohesive interactions on the mechanical resilience of composites, with notable increases in Young's modulus and toughness occurring with increasing reinforcement volume and size.
elements.sent-2411-300: These findings suggest that the size and reinforcement proportion directly dictate the fracture mechanics of the composites, impacting their toughness and resistance to crack propagation, especially in global softening regions where fiber-matrix interfaces concentrate stress.
elements.sent-2411-301: Despite the exciting results obtained, this approach currently omits temperature-dependent fracture behavior, and MD simulations rely on empirical interatomic potentials.
elements.sent-2411-302: Future work could incorporate temperature-coupled cohesive fracture models with machine learning interatomic potentials , thereby enabling the investigation of fracture mechanics under thermal variations in heterostructures and nanocomposites.
elements.sent-2411-303: Furthermore, the proposed model does not take into account the flexible nature of nanosheets, which requires the construction of more complex RVEs.
elements.sent-2411-304: The transversely isotropic mechanical nature of layered two-dimensional materials is also not considered, which can be investigated in future studies.

elements.sent-2411-325: The detailed derivations of the degradation function $g(d)$ with a polynomial order of $p=2$ with respect to the phase-field variable $d$ are expressed as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{split} g'(d) = - \frac{(1 - d)^2 (-2 \, (1 - d) + a_1 \, d \, (a_2 + 2 \, a_3 \, d) + a_1 \, (1 + a_2 \, d + a_3 \, d^2))}{((1 - d)^2 + a_1 \, d \, (1 + a_2 \, d + a_3 \, d^2))^2} - \frac{2 \, (1 - d)}{(1 - d)^2 + a_1 \, d \, (1 + a_2 \, d + a_3 \, d^2)} g'(d) = \frac{a_1 \, (d - 1) (1 + d + 2 \, a_2 \, d + 3 \, a_3 \, d^2 - a_3 \, d^3)}{(1 + (a_1 - 2) \, d + (1 + a_1 \, a_2) d^2 + a_1 \, a_3 d^3)^2}. \end{split}\end{equation}.
